primary_diagnosis.name: Acute mechanical overuse flare of mild seronegative inflammatory (rheumatoid) arthritis

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has a background mild seronegative inflammatory (rheumatoid-pattern) arthritis affecting the small joints of the hands — synovial inflammation of the MCP and PIP joints without the aggressive erosive destruction or high-titre autoantibodies (RF/CCP negative) of classic rheumatoid arthritis. An unusually heavy day of manual hand use mechanically provoked an acute synovitis flare: overuse increases local synovial inflammatory mediator load, producing pain, soft-tissue swelling and functional loss out of proportion to the underlying disease severity. The dramatic acuity and the patient's catastrophic fear of permanent deformity make the flare LOOK like an aggressive infective or systemic process, but the distribution (symmetrical, polyarticular), the absence of fever, erythema and joint heat, intact passive range of motion, and short-duration morning stiffness all point to a benign inflammatory/mechanical flare rather than septic arthritis. Flares of this kind are typically short-lived and respond to simple anti-inflammatory therapy and rest.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of mild seronegative inflammatory arthritis (overuse-provoked)
differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical small-joint hand pain and stiffness on a background of chronic intermittent finger aches, provoked by heavy manual use.
Acute severe joint pain and swelling always mandates active exclusion of joint infection — a joint emergency — before attributing to a benign flare.
Acute painful joint swelling can be crystal-induced; usually monoarticular and severe.
Viral infection can cause a symmetrical small-joint polyarthropathy — screen for a preceding viral prodrome/rash.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the AI Doctor should hold a broad differential and explicitly keep septic arthritis in play — NOT anchor prematurely on either a benign flare or a catastrophic infection.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of mild seronegative inflammatory arthritis (overuse-provoked)
differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical polyarticular MCP/PIP involvement, short (<30 min) morning stiffness, chronic intermittent background, clear overuse trigger, afebrile.
Made less likely by afebrile, symmetrical and polyarticular pattern, but not yet formally excluded on history alone — retain as safety net.
Symmetrical multi-joint pattern rather than a classic severe monoarthritis; no tophi reported.
No preceding viral prodrome, rash or systemic symptoms.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history should shift the balance strongly toward a benign inflammatory/mechanical flare while KEEPING the septic-arthritis safety net active.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of mild seronegative inflammatory arthritis (overuse-provoked)
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, no single hot/red joint, symmetrical polyarticular swelling — the benign flare picture is coherent and the red flags screen negative.
No fever/chills, no single erythematous hot fluctuant joint, symmetrical distribution — clinically excluded at presentation, but remains the key safety-net trigger if a joint becomes acutely hot/red.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-flag screen the correct posture is confident-but-safety-netted: manage as a benign flare via telehealth, and explicitly retain the septic-joint escalation trigger. Over-triage to ED at this point is an error of commission.

differential.diagnosis: Acute mechanical overuse flare of mild seronegative inflammatory arthritis
differential.evidence_basis: Full picture: chronic intermittent symmetrical small-joint disease, overuse-provoked acute synovitis, afebrile, no septic features, functionally impaired but not a joint emergency. Presumptive on telehealth pending GP/rheumatology confirmation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Presumptive telehealth diagnosis; a definitive label and long-term plan (NSAID/DMARD, rheumatology referral) require GP follow-up. Cannot be confirmed remotely.

red_flags.description: A single joint that is intensely red, hot, swollen and exquisitely painful on the slightest movement (features of acute septic arthritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Septic arthritis is a joint emergency; a monoarticular hot red joint with severe pain on micro-motion must be assumed infected until proven otherwise and needs urgent ED assessment and joint aspiration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed joint infection leads to rapid cartilage destruction, permanent joint damage and potential systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Fever, chills or systemic illness accompanying the joint symptoms
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever with acute arthritis raises the probability of septic arthritis or a systemic inflammatory/infective process and mandates urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to act on fever with joint infection risks sepsis and irreversible joint destruction.

red_flags.description: Progressive spread to large joints (knees, shoulders) or swelling persisting unchanged beyond a week despite anti-inflammatories
red_flags.clinical_significance: Evolution beyond a self-limited small-joint flare suggests a more active or aggressive inflammatory arthritis requiring GP/rheumatology reassessment and possible disease-modifying therapy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of a progressive inflammatory arthritis can allow avoidable joint damage.

red_flags.description: Exquisite pain on passive micro-motion of a joint (a bedside septic-arthritis sign)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe pain on tiny passive movement is characteristic of an infected or crystal-inflamed joint; here passive range of motion was intact, arguing strongly against septic arthritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking this sign in a truly infected joint delays emergency care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the intensity of the pain and the patient's panic and escalating a benign symmetrical flare to ED or an ambulance — the exact unnecessary emergency presentation the consult should prevent.
Anchoring on 'acute severe hot-sounding joints = septic arthritis' without weighting the discriminators (afebrile, symmetrical, polyarticular, no erythema/heat, intact passive motion).
The inverse error — reassuring so completely that the septic-joint safety net is dropped, so an evolving single hot joint or new fever later is not acted upon.
Declaring a confirmed diagnosis remotely; joint warmth, fluctuance, passive micro-motion pain and serology cannot be assessed via chat — the label is presumptive pending in-person review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for joint warmth, fluctuance or effusion
Whether passive micro-motion of a joint provokes exquisite pain (a septic-arthritis sign)
Objective vital signs (temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate)
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) and full blood count
Rheumatoid factor / anti-CCP serology (historically negative, but not re-confirmable remotely)
Synovial fluid analysis if a joint were to become acutely hot
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage symptomatically via telehealth with a clear septic-arthritis safety net; escalate to the emergency department (T4) only if a single joint becomes hot/red/exquisitely painful or fever develops; arrange a routine GP review within about 7 days for confirmation, rheumatology referral and a long-term plan.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The risk runs in BOTH directions: over-calling a septic joint and sending the patient to ED unnecessarily, or falsely reassuring and missing an evolving hot joint. The mechanical/inflammatory nature cannot be confirmed remotely, so the diagnosis is presumptive only.
